issue_body:
The following cookiecutter change:

   https://github.com/inveniosoftware/cookiecutter-invenio-module/pull/98

should be propagated to this Invenio module.

Namely, in `run-tests.sh`, the sphinx for doctests is invoked after pytest run:

```console
$ tail -3 ./\{\{\ cookiecutter.project_shortname\ \}\}/run-tests.sh
sphinx-build -qnNW docs docs/_build/html && python setup.py test && sphinx-build -qnNW -b doctest docs docs/_build/doctest
```

This sometimes led to problems on Travis CI with the second sphinx-build run due
to "disappearing" dependencies after the example application was tested.

A solution that worked for invenio-marc21 (see
https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio-marc21/pull/49#issuecomment-281287103)
and that was integrated in cookiecutter (see
https://github.com/inveniosoftware/cookiecutter-invenio-module/pull/98) was to
run doctest execution in pytest, removing the second `sphinx-build` invocation.

This both solved Travis CI build failures and simplified test suite execution.

Note that this change may necessitate to amend the code tests etc so that things
would be executed with the Flask application context (see
https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio-marc21/pull/49/commits/09e98fc18fd5591dc65d806286e8da51b7e384b4).

issue_title: Add ability to include the hostname in lockfile in addition to the PID
issue_body:
In a container environment (e.g. Docker), the PID is typically always identical even if multiple containers are running under the same operating system instance.

Clearly, inspecting lock files doesn't then help much in debugging. It would be very useful to be able to identify the container which created the lock file. Since Docker uses the container identifier or name as the hostname, this information can be stored in the lock file in addition to or instead of the PID.

issue_body:
In https://bugs.launchpad.net/zope.deprecation/+bug/1359032, @mesozoic reported:

> Calling deprecated() on a function which takes no args will result in a wrapped call that will raise the following exception:

    TypeError: deprecated_method() takes at least 1 argument (0 given)

Mandrill supplies a header `X-Mandrill-Signature` to [authenticate their requests](https://mandrill.zendesk.com/hc/en-us/articles/205583257-How-to-Authenticate-Webhook-Requests). Inbound Email should be able to support it by adding one setting to determine the webhook url.

Mandrill's signature calculation is as follows:

1. Create a string with the webhook's URL, exactly as you entered it in Mandrill (including any query strings, if applicable). Mandrill always signs webhook requests with the exact URL you provided when you configured the webhook. A difference as small as including or removing a trailing slash will prevent the signature from validating.
2. Sort the request's POST variables alphabetically by key.
3. Append each POST variable's key and value to the URL string, with no delimiter.
4. Hash the resulting string with HMAC-SHA1, using your webhook's authentication key to generate a binary signature.
5. Base64 encode the binary signature
6. Compare the binary signature that you generated to the signature provided in the X-Mandrill-Signature HTTP header.

issue_title: viewbox parameter in osm backend is used wrongly
issue_body:
There are two serious issues with your use of the viewbox parameter in the nominatim backend:

1. It is wrongly formatted. There are brackets around the coordinates which cause some random interpretation of the coordinates on the server side.
2. The viewbox parameter is always added. This is bad because the viewbox parameter changes the search query to a geographically restricted query. It changes which results are shown and in which order they appear. The fact that the default spans the entire planet means that you get the results pretty much a random order instead in order of their importance. viewbox should only be added when explicitly requested.

The `breadcrumbs` module uses the `logging._srcfile` attribute and crashes if it is `None`. The `logging` module sort of allows this, though:

> \# _srcfile is only used in conjunction with sys._getframe().
> \# To provide compatibility with older versions of Python, set _srcfile
> \# to None if _getframe() is not available; this value will prevent
> \# findCaller() from being called.

We use a third party library that actively sets `logging._srcfile` to `None`. I'm aware that asking raven to consider this is rather weird, but I thought I'd at least ask :smile:

issue_title: NamespaceManager.bind(prefix, namespace, replace=True) leaves existing prefix in place
issue_body:
I want to change an existing and bound namespace prefix. From the documentation of NamespaceManager I understand that "bind" with parameter "replace=True" should achieve this. However, while this does associate the namespace with the new prefix, the old prefix remains bound to the same namespace.
This can make it impossible to obtain a serialization with the new prefix. Is this intended behaviour?

Code example:

 ```python
from rdflib import Graph, Namespace

g = Graph()

g.parse(data="""<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<rdf:RDF
   xmlns:ns1="http://somewhere/else#"
   xmlns:ns2="http://nowhere/else#"
   xmlns:rdf="http://www.w3.org/1999/02/22-rdf-syntax-ns#"
>
  <rdf:Description rdf:about="http://somewhere/else#y">
    <ns2:Q rdf:resource="http://somewhere/else#z"/>
  </rdf:Description>
  <rdf:Description rdf:about="http://somewhere/else#root">
    <ns1:P rdf:resource="http://somewhere/else#y"/>
    <ns1:P rdf:resource="http://somewhere/else#x"/>
  </rdf:Description>
</rdf:RDF>
""")

for ns in g.namespaces():
    print('%s (%s)' % (ns))
    
# prints:
#
# xml (http://www.w3.org/XML/1998/namespace)
# ns2 (http://nowhere/else#)
# ns1 (http://somewhere/else#)
# rdfs (http://www.w3.org/2000/01/rdf-schema#)
# xsd (http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema#)
# rdf (http://www.w3.org/1999/02/22-rdf-syntax-ns#)
#
# (as expected)

print(g.serialize().decode())

# prints:
#
# <?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
# <rdf:RDF
#    xmlns:ns1="http://somewhere/else#"
#    xmlns:ns2="http://nowhere/else#"
#    xmlns:rdf="http://www.w3.org/1999/02/22-rdf-syntax-ns#"
# >
#   <rdf:Description rdf:about="http://somewhere/else#y">
#     <ns2:Q rdf:resource="http://somewhere/else#z"/>
#   </rdf:Description>
#   <rdf:Description rdf:about="http://somewhere/else#root">
#     <ns1:P rdf:resource="http://somewhere/else#y"/>
#     <ns1:P rdf:resource="http://somewhere/else#x"/>
#   </rdf:Description>
# </rdf:RDF>
#
# (as expected)

elseNS = Namespace('http://somewhere/else#') # same namespace as ns1 above

g.namespace_manager.bind('there', elseNS, replace=True) # attempt to rebind to other prefix

for ns in g.namespaces():
    print('%s (%s)' % (ns))    
    
# prints:
#
# xsd (http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema#)
# there (http://somewhere/else#)
# rdfs (http://www.w3.org/2000/01/rdf-schema#)
# rdf (http://www.w3.org/1999/02/22-rdf-syntax-ns#)
# xml (http://www.w3.org/XML/1998/namespace)
# ns2 (http://nowhere/else#)
# ns1 (http://somewhere/else#)
#
# ("there" and "ns1" are bound to the same namespace)

print(g.serialize().decode())

# prints:
#
# <?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
# <rdf:RDF
#    xmlns:ns1="http://somewhere/else#"
#    xmlns:ns2="http://nowhere/else#"
#    xmlns:rdf="http://www.w3.org/1999/02/22-rdf-syntax-ns#"
# >
#   <rdf:Description rdf:about="http://somewhere/else#root">
#     <ns1:P rdf:resource="http://somewhere/else#x"/>
#     <ns1:P rdf:resource="http://somewhere/else#y"/>
#   </rdf:Description>
#   <rdf:Description rdf:about="http://somewhere/else#y">
#     <ns2:Q rdf:resource="http://somewhere/else#z"/>
#   </rdf:Description>
# </rdf:RDF>
#
# (in the serialization "ns1" is used, not "there" as was intended)

issue_title: translateUpdate does not convert relative URIs when using non-HTTP base
issue_body:
I want to update a graph with a user-provided SPARQL-Update string that may contain relative URIs. In order to do that, I am pre-processing the string using `parseUpdate` (AFAIK that is a way to do that, not sure if it's the only one or the best one).

When parsing an update string, I noticed that if the base URI is HTTP-based, all goes well. Not so if the base is a URN: 

    >>> upd_str = 'DELETE {} INSERT {<#h1> <http://ex.org/ns/p1> <http:ex.org/ns/o1> } WHERE {}'
    >>> translateUpdate(parseUpdate(upd_str), URIRef('http://ex.org/gr/a'))
    [CompValue([('delete',
                 CompValue([('quads', defaultdict(list, {})), ('triples', [])])),
                ('insert',
                 CompValue([('quads', defaultdict(list, {})),
                            ('triples',
                             [(rdflib.term.URIRef('http://ex.org/gr/a#h1'),
                               rdflib.term.URIRef('http://ex.org/ns/p1'),
                               rdflib.term.URIRef('http:ex.org/ns/o1'))])])),
                ('where', CompValue([('triples', [])]))])]
    >>> translateUpdate(parseUpdate(upd_str), URIRef('urn:ns:a'))
    [CompValue([('delete',
                 CompValue([('quads', defaultdict(list, {})), ('triples', [])])),
                ('insert',
                 CompValue([('quads', defaultdict(list, {})),
                            ('triples',
                             [(rdflib.term.URIRef('#h1'),
                               rdflib.term.URIRef('http://ex.org/ns/p1'),
                               rdflib.term.URIRef('http:ex.org/ns/o1'))])])),
                ('where', CompValue([('triples', [])]))])]

Note that in the result of the second `translateUpdate` the `#h1` URI is not absolutized.

issue_title: Improve headers detection logic when all columns are of "string" type
issue_body:
Consider a file like this:
```
A,B,C
D,E,F
```
Since all fields (and headers) are of type "string", we assume `header = true` printing a verbose message that "no better guess is possible". However it IS possible to have a better guess, by testing whether there are any repeating values within each column in the first 100 rows. For example, if the file is
```
a,b,c,d
a,h,i,j
a,e,c,
z,e,n,m
```
then we can reasonably assume that the first row is data, since it would be very uncommon for a column header to have the same content as one of the cell values.

An example file: https://s3.amazonaws.com/h2o-public-test-data/fread/Mushroom.gz

This will allow them to work with the the built-in `copy` and `pickle` modules (note that pure-Python classes should for the most part already work with these modules, unless they contain Boost.Python objects as attributes).

issue_title: Copy f-string line/col fix from CPython
issue_body:
There's an issue with f-string line/column numbers (https://github.com/python/mypy/issues/3562) which was reported for mypy but is actually an ast bug.

It's been fixed upstream in https://github.com/python/cpython/pull/1800 ([bpo-30465](https://bugs.python.org/issue30465)) so the fix should be incorporated here so we can eventually declare victory in mypy.

issue_title: Don't send back entire schema after validation errors
issue_body:
### Description

When Connexion validates incoming data and finds a property is missing, it will dutifully report this back to the caller. However, it will include the entire swagger spec/schema as part of the response. For large APIs, this can be tens of KB of text, which is not ideal for debugging or logging.

### Expected behaviour

Expect to only receive the error portion, eg: "Missing required property 'email'"

### Actual behaviour

I receive the error portion following by a long JSON string that is my Swagger spec.

### Steps to reproduce

The actual generation of this error appears to occur in *jsonschema*, but this can be solved within Connexion by only sending back the `.message` attribute in the `problem()` call, rather than the string-representation of the entire exception object.

    def validate_schema(self, data):
        """
        :type schema: dict
        :rtype: flask.Response | None
        """
        if self.is_null_value_valid and is_null(data):
            return None

        try:
            validate(data, self.schema, format_checker=draft4_format_checker)
        except ValidationError as exception:
            logger.error("%s validation error: %s" % (flask.request.url, exception))
            return problem(400, 'Bad Request', str(exception))

        return None

Instead, the second-to-last line could read:

            return problem(400, 'Bad Request', exception.message)

Which will return the expected (and succinct) error message without the Swagger spec included.


### Additional info:

Output of the commands:

- `python --version`
  - Python 3.5.1

- `pip show connexion | grep "^Version\:"`
  - Version: 1.0.88

### Description
Returning an instance of `connexion.problem` from an error handler results in an application crash in `connexion == 1.1.9`

### Expected behaviour (`connexion == 1.0.129`)

Returning a nice error like:
```
HTTP/1.0 500 INTERNAL SERVER ERROR
Content-Type: application/problem+json
Content-Length: 129
Server: Werkzeug/0.12.2 Python/3.6.1

{
  "type": "about:blank",
  "title": "Internal Server Error",
  "detail": "Could not connect to LDAP server",
  "status": 500
}
``` 

### Actual behaviour in (`connexion == 1.1.9`)

Stacktrace 
```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/lib/python3.6/site-packages/flask/app.py", line 1997, in __call__
    return self.wsgi_app(environ, start_response)
  File "/lib/python3.6/site-packages/flask/app.py", line 1985, in wsgi_app
    response = self.handle_exception(e)
  File "/lib/python3.6/site-packages/flask/app.py", line 1540, in handle_exception
    reraise(exc_type, exc_value, tb)
  File "/lib/python3.6/site-packages/flask/_compat.py", line 33, in reraise
    raise value
  File "/lib/python3.6/site-packages/flask/app.py", line 1982, in wsgi_app
    response = self.full_dispatch_request()
  File "/lib/python3.6/site-packages/flask/app.py", line 1615, in full_dispatch_request
    return self.finalize_request(rv)
  File "/lib/python3.6/site-packages/flask/app.py", line 1630, in finalize_request
    response = self.make_response(rv)
  File "/lib/python3.6/site-packages/flask/app.py", line 1740, in make_response
    rv = self.response_class.force_type(rv, request.environ)
  File "/lib/python3.6/site-packages/werkzeug/wrappers.py", line 885, in force_type
    response = BaseResponse(*_run_wsgi_app(response, environ))
  File "/lib/python3.6/site-packages/werkzeug/wrappers.py", line 57, in _run_wsgi_app
    return _run_wsgi_app(*args)
  File "/lib/python3.6/site-packages/werkzeug/test.py", line 884, in run_wsgi_app
    app_rv = app(environ, start_response)
TypeError: 'ConnexionResponse' object is not callable
```

### Steps to reproduce

1. Define handler: 
```
def ldap_connection_error(the_error):
    return connexion.problem(status=500, title=http.client.responses[500], detail='Could not connect to LDAP server')
```

2. Add the handler to the app:
```
    app = connexion.App(__name__, specification_dir='./swagger/')
    app.add_error_handler(LDAPSocketOpenError, error_handlers.ldap_connection_error) 
```

3. Raise the corresponding exception and the app will crash.


### Additional info:

Output of the commands:

- `python --version`
`Python 3.6.1`
- `pip show connexion | grep "^Version\:"`
`Version: 1.0.129` / `Version: 1.1.9`

### Description
Requested datetime.timeinterval objects returned by the DB are not supported and throw an error.

### Expected bahaviour
Return JSON from timedelta objects

### Actual behaviour
Server will throw a 500 with "TypeError: datetime.timedelta() is not JSON serializable"

### Steps to reproduce
Commit a timedelta object to a db, request it.

### Additional info:

Python 3.5.2
Connexion 1.0.129

issue_title: Passed in configuration values are overridden by the defaults
issue_body:
I wanted to override the default log level sent to Sentry, so I decided to set `logging=False` and pass that into the wrapper. But I noticed that Sentry was still receiving INFO messages and when [digging around the code I noticed this][update]:

```python
self.config.update(config_defaults)
```

It seems like it should be the other way around to work, `config_defaults` should be updated with `self.config`. 

Right now I'm working around this by setting the environment variable `SENTRY_CAPTURE_LOGS` to an empty string since that's falsy in Python, but I see there's another PR that would let me pass in false instead. :)

[update]: https://github.com/Netflix-Skunkworks/raven-python-lambda/blob/master/raven_python_lambda/__init__.py#L102

issue_title: Create a new version of the variables endpoint
issue_body:
# Proposition 1

## `/variables` endpoint

List of all variables as an object mapping their variable name to objects containing a `description` property whose value is their `label`, matching the `/parameters` endpoint.

Each variable is then exposed under the `/variable/{{name}}` URL as an object whose values are documented below.

## InputVariable

### Before

```json

	"default": 0,
	"entity": "individu",
	"label": "Salaire de base, en général appelé salaire brut, la 1ère ligne sur la fiche de paie",
	"start_line_number": 479,
	"source_code": "class salaire_de_base(Variable):\n    column = FloatCol\n    entity = Individu\n    label = u\"Salaire de base, en général appelé salaire brut, la 1ère ligne sur la fiche de paie\"\n    set_input = set_input_divide_by_period\n    url = u'http://www.insee.fr/fr/methodes/default.asp?page=definitions/salaire-mensuel-base-smb.htm'\n    definition_period = MONTH\n",
	"source_file_path": "model/revenus/activite/salarie.py",
	"name": "salaire_de_base",
	"url": "http://www.insee.fr/fr/methodes/default.asp?page=definitions/salaire-mensuel-base-smb.htm"
},
```

### After

"salaire_de_base": {
	"description": "Salaire de base, en général appelé salaire brut, la 1ère ligne sur la fiche de paie",
	"valueType": "Float",
	"defaultValue": 0,
	"definitionPeriod": "month",
	"entity": "individu",
	"reference": "http://www.insee.fr/fr/methodes/default.asp?page=definitions/salaire-mensuel-base-smb.htm",
	"source": "https://github.com/openfisca/openfisca-france/master/model/revenus/activite/salarie.py#L479-489"
},
```

## Variable

### Before

```json
{
	"@type": "Float",
	"default": 0,
	"entity": "famille",
	"label": "Allocations logements",
	"start_line_number": 354,
	"source_code": "class aides_logement(Variable):\n    column = FloatCol\n    entity = Famille\n    label = u\"Allocations logements\"\n    url = \"http://vosdroits.service-public.fr/particuliers/N20360.xhtml\"\n    definition_period = YEAR\n\n    def function(famille, period):\n        '''\n        Prestations logement\n        '''\n        apl = famille('apl', period, options = [ADD])\n        als = famille('als', period, options = [ADD])\n        alf = famille('alf', period, options = [ADD])\n        crds_logement = famille('crds_logement', period, options = [ADD])\n\n        return apl + als + alf + crds_logement\n",

		"@type": "SimpleFormula",

	}
},
```

	"description": "Allocations logements",

	"defaultValue": 0,
	"definitionPeriod": "month",
	"entity": "famille",

		}
	}
},
```

### Before

```json
{
	"@type": "Float",

	"label": "Revenu de référence, basé sur la situation familiale, pris en compte dans le calcul des AL.",

2)\n            )\n\n        R2 = pfam_n_2.af.bmaf * (\n            al.r2.taux3_dom * residence_dom * (al_nb_pac == 1) +\n            al.r2.personnes_isolees_ou_couples_avec_2_enf * (al_nb_pac >= 2) +\n            al.r2.majoration_par_enf_supp_a_charge * (al_nb_pac > 2) * (al_nb_pac - 2)\n            )\n\n        R0 = round_(12 * (R1 - R2) * (1 - al.autres.abat_sal))\n\n        return R0\n\n    # cf Décret n° 2014-1739 du 29 décembre 2014 relatif au calcul des aides personnelles au logement\n    @dated_function(start = date(2015, 1, 1))\n    def function_2015(famille, period, legislation):\n        al = legislation(period).prestations.aides_logement\n        couple = famille('al_couple', period)\n        al_nb_pac = famille('al_nb_personnes_a_charge', period)\n\n        R0 = (\n            al.R0.taux_seul * not_(couple) * (al_nb_pac == 0) +\n            al.R0.taux_couple * couple * (al_nb_pac == 0) +\n            al.R0.taux1pac * (al_nb_pac == 1) +\n            al.R0.taux2pac * (al_nb_pac == 2) +\n            al.R0.taux3pac * (al_nb_pac == 3) +\n            al.R0.taux4pac * (al_nb_pac == 4) +\n            al.R0.taux5pac * (al_nb_pac == 5) +\n            al.R0.taux6pac * (al_nb_pac == 6) +\n            al.R0.taux_pac_supp * (al_nb_pac > 6) * (al_nb_pac - 6)\n            )\n\n        return R0\n",
	"source_file_path": "model/prestations/aides_logement.py",
	"name": "aide_logement_R0",
	"formula": {

			{

		},

		}

},
``` 

## Notes

- The `comments` of the formulas is empty for all but 6 items, 5 of which are TODOs, the last one being an improperly-placed label (PR incoming to fix this). We can drop it.
- The `doc` of the formulas is mostly misplaced labels with duplicate date information. We can drop it from the API, the data should be normalised into the labels.
- We are not 100% satisfied with the `source` property name. `href` would be ambiguous since it would make the user leave the realm of the API itself.
- The parsers do not currently expose the individual dated functions source code.
- Many formulas have no start date. We default to `datetime.date.min`. We are not sure whether this information should be added by the API or set as a default for all formulas within each tax and benefits system, with the API simply exposing the data.
- We remove the `class` source code, since all the information it contains is extracted and exposed as properties.

The replacement will be handled by a string when dataset ID is explicitly needed (which will only be for power users). In order to make use easier, they will also have versions in `__init__` which can use a default / implicit connection.

Steps to complete:

- [ ] Make `set_default_connection` similar to [`set_default_dataset`][1]
- [ ] Remove `query()`, `entity()`, and `transaction()` methods from `Dataset`, replace their use elsewhere.
- [ ] Move `Dataset.get_entity` to `Key` (will impact elsewhere, e.g. `Entity.reload`)

It appears to me that if I want to scan through all rows of a Bigtable, [`Table.read_rows()`](https://googlecloudplatform.github.io/gcloud-python/stable/bigtable-table.html#gcloud.bigtable.table.Table.read_rows) is the function to use. This returns a [PartialRowsData](https://googlecloudplatform.github.io/gcloud-python/stable/bigtable-row-data.html#gcloud.bigtable.row_data.PartialRowsData).

Why is `PartialRowsData` not an iterable? I'd like to consume all the rows for a "hello bigtable" sample by iterating through them. Something like

```
print('Scan for all greetings:')
for row in table.read_rows():
    print(row.cells[COLUMN_FAMILY_NAME][COLUMN_NAME.encode('UTF-8')][0].decode('UTF-8'))
```

Instead, it appears that I need to use a while loop and watch a dictionary that gets populated. I think this means that even if I don't need to store all the rows in memory at once, they still do. That's both inefficient and not following Python idioms (of generators / iterables).

issue_title: Cloud Spanner Client should prevent nested transactions
issue_labels: [{'id': 539494117, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/labels/api:%20spanner', 'name': 'api: spanner', 'color': 'cd7f32', 'default': False}, {'id': 544604244, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/labels/priority:%20p1', 'name': 'priority: p1', 'color': 'ffa03e', 'default': False}, {'id': 544604235, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/labels/status:%20acknowledged', 'name': 'status: acknowledged', 'color': 'fef2c0', 'default': False}, {'id': 486182624, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/labels/status:%20release%20blocking', 'name': 'status: release blocking', 'color': 'ffee58', 'default': False}, {'id': 77438582, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/labels/type:%20enhancement', 'name': 'type: enhancement', 'color': '8bc34a', 'default': False}]
issue_body:
Cloud Spanner does not support [nested transactions](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nested_transaction). But the client library does allow users to call `database.run_in_transaction` inside of the callback that is provided to an outer `database.run_in_transaction`. This is misleading since users might believe that this will behave like a nested transaction but in reality these will be two independent transactions. This is confusing and a source of bugs. Specifically the inner transaction might succeed while the outer might ABORT, in which case the callback will be rerun thus rerunning the inner transaction. 
We should prevent this from happening by detecting and raising an error if someone calls `database.run_in_transaction` inside the callback provided to `database.run_in_transaction`.  Note that this needs to be done before Beta since this is a known breaking change.
Also note that if we come across a use case where we do want to allow calling a nested `database.run_in_transaction`, we should be able to enable that in future without making a breaking change. 

cc @jgeewax @bjwatson

issue_title: storage API: Allow wildcard in object path to allow retrieval of several objects
issue_body:
[gcsfs](https://github.com/dask/gcsfs) has a very handy feature that allows you to fetch multiple files by allowing wildcards in the object path. I think this would be a nice little feature to add to this library.

This example illustrates the idea:

```python 
from google.cloud import storage
c = storage.Client()
bucket = c.bucket('mybucket')
blobs = bucket.blob('2017/*.csv')
```

As far as I know, the current way to accomplish the same would be to filter the list of all the files in the bucket  and then fetch the files one by one (please correct me if I'm wrong :). The problem with this is that it's slow if you have a bucket with a very large number of files.

Cheers,
Halfdan

issue_title: Bigtable: reports of transient failures in long running scans

issue_title: Pants should handle javac & scalac compiler plugins that emit non-class files
issue_body:
This was originally filed here: https://github.com/twitter/commons/issues/207

Currently pants both tightly relies on mapping inputs to outputs and re-arranging target processing within dependency constraints.  For javac and scala processing this means that jmake and zinc are leaned on to give full source file to classfile mappings and targets are passed in chunks to each compiler to amortize overheads in calling out to the compilers.  As a result any non-class files emitted in javac or scalac compile rounds are untracked and untrackable by pants in principle.  Although they could be detected via an expensive fs walk, they could not be associated with the owning target in the common multi-target chunk case.

Pants should provide an api for jvm compiler plugins to convey non-classfile input/output mapping data.

The proposal is to establish a well known resource file with a prescribed format - here's the 1st draft:
location: [classfile uoutput dir]/META-INF/pants/resource-mappings.txt 
format:
  * 1 entry per line
  * each line: [absolute source file path] ' -> ' [absolute output file path]
  * Blank lines and lines with leading # (comment lines) are skipped

import jose
from time import time
from Crypto.PublicKey import RSA

# key for demonstration purposes

claims = {
	'iss': 'http://www.example.com',

}

issue_title: API Gateway Authorizers Insecure Authentication Token Logging
issue_body:
The API Gateway default templates are logging the client's authentication token into the CloudWatch logs. While this may be helpful when debugging the function, this should be avoided in production as it leaks valid user tokens to the log files, which can be seen by users that likely should not have access to invoke all of the APIs on behalf of that user.

Yes, folks should restrict access to their log files. But, it is highly unlikely that all users viewing logs should be able to impersonate all users across the API using their token. I have seen the token logging code left in place in several security reviews recently, and the log data could have result in a major incident.

Here are 2 instances that I identified (there could be more):

https://github.com/awslabs/serverless-application-model/blob/a09929bcdd7cd6083d5c344ce5a64208a56bc4a6/examples/apps/api-gateway-authorizer-nodejs/index.js#L315

issue_title: device_names not complete due to parsing bug in FfmpegFormat.Reader._get_cam_inputname()
issue_body:
Issue:

I am using imageio version 2.2.0 with ffmpeg v3.2.4 on a Windows 10 laptop with an onboard webcam and a video grabber attached through usb. A direct call to ffmpeg lists both video devices, but `imageio.get_reader('<video1>')` raises an IndexError.

Origin:

I traced this back to the `device_list` in `FfmpegFormat.Reader._get_cam_inputname()`, which contains only one device instead of two.

Cause:

This is caused by an assumption in the parsing algorithm.

Line 264 in ffmpeg.py relies on `"` being present at the start of a `line` containing a device name: 
    `if line.startswith('"'): ...`

However, ffmpeg v3.2.4 (on my system) also returns 'Alternative name: "......"' (see file [ffmpeg_device_list.txt](https://github.com/imageio/imageio/files/1321996/ffmpeg_device_list.txt)), which causes `in_video_devices` to be set to `False`. As it is never set `True` again, all subsequent video devices are skipped from the list.

Solution:

A patch that works for me is the following:
```
...
# Parse the result
device_names = []
in_video_devices = False
for line in infos.splitlines():
    if line.startswith('[dshow'):
        line = line.split(']', 1)[1].strip()
        if in_video_devices and line.startswith('"'):
            device_names.append(line[1:-1])
        elif 'video devices' in line:
            in_video_devices = True
        elif 'audio devices' in line:
            in_video_devices = False
...
```
Although I am not sure this covers all possible cases.

## Summary

When looking at the summaries for channels with little or no existing activity, the summaries don't include accurate information about the contents of the channel.

In particular, the `progress` array only contains overviews for content types that have had at least one recorded user interaction with them. This means that (for example) the number of videos in the channel might be reported as `0` if none have been watched yet.

issue_title: Python API - bug caused by compatibility issues with the tables module
issue_body:
I got this error using the method createMapping() from the Python API:

```
--> 172         mymap = self.createArray(self.root.lookup, title, atom=tables.UInt32Atom(),AttributeError: 'File' object has no attribute 'createArray'`
It turns out that in my "tables" module (version 3.3.0) the method createArray() is called crate_array().
```

I fixed this bug localy by manually changing one line in the `createMapping(self, title, entries, overwrite=False)` method of the `File` class.

The original code was:
```
# Write the mapping!
mymap = self.createArray(self.root.lookup, title, atom=tables.UInt32Atom(),
shape=(len(entries),) )
```
I replaced it by:

```
# Write the mapping!
mymap = self.create_array(self.root.lookup, title, atom=tables.UInt32Atom(),
shape=(len(entries),) )
```

For information, I am using Python 2.7.3, and the openmatrix version 0.2
Is this repo missing a license.txt?  The old Google Code archive says GNU Lesser GPL, but I don't see it here.

issue_title: Generated relation and storage hooks are broken in 1.10
issue_body: I've just built a reactive charm with 1.10, and had errors in storage-attached due to the generated hook file having a blank line before the shebang. Probably broken with the change over to using Python 3.

issue_title: Support rebuilding charms with locked wheelhouse
issue_body: In order to support patching a stable charm, we need a way to rebuild a charm but not update the wheelhouse of Python dependencies.  If `charm-build` generates a `wheelhouse.lock` file with specific revs and SHAs that we picked up during the build, then you could pass that in to `--wheelhouse-overrides` to rebuild with those exactly.  We might even look into [pipfile](https://github.com/pypa/pipfile) which has built-in support for generating those lock files.

issue_title: Layers can only use https: urls
issue_body: Per https://bugs.launchpad.net/leadership-layer/+bug/1539142 , charm-build doesn't know how to handle layers resolving to git: or git+ssh: urls. https: seems to be the only option.

issue_title: Can't delete bundles in worker_offline state

issue_body:
Just ran into pyamg; great to see these complex algorithms be made available in such a neat package!

I started to try out pyamg in some of the more complex solves in my project, and these gave the following results; which perhaps could be improved upon by those with more knowhow of amg; and would perhaps be interesting additions to the examples/benchmarks.

The first thing I tried was a darcy-flow with high anisotropy; like groundwater that is free to move in some cracks but not in other places. [runnable example here](https://github.com/EelcoHoogendoorn/pycomplex/blob/master/examples/flow/darcy_flow.py). Even for modest grid sizes this solve takes several tens of seconds. This strikes me as the killer-app of amg; but I havnt been able to realize anything but modest gains so far; about 20% over a minres solve. That strikes me as way below expectations. Maybe it is because I am trying to solve it as a mixed pressure-velocity formulation?

The other thing I tried is more or less an exact copy-paste from the poisson eigenmodes example. [runnable example here](https://github.com/EelcoHoogendoorn/pycomplex/blob/master/examples/harmonics/spherical.py)
Here I am getting some decent gains for highly refined grids; but for refinement=6 grids and K=200 I get this error (note that there are perfectly well formed laplacian operators). Any ideas?

`numpy.linalg.linalg.LinAlgError: the leading minor of order 376 of 'b' is not positive definite. The factorization of 'b' could not be completed and no eigenvalues or eigenvectors were computed.
`

habituated CR is implemented. concurrent CR is needed in cr.py

Migrated from http://code.google.com/p/pyamg/issues/detail?id=77

issue_title: How can I ignore the certificate validation?
issue_body:
I have a test firewall to try pandevice without any valid SSL certificate. In order to work with XML APIs of Palo Alto, I use curl -k -X <url>.

I am trying to use pandevice.

pandevice.errors.PanURLError: URLError: reason: [SSL: CERTIFICATE_VERIFY_FAILED] certificate verify failed (_ssl.c:579)

I guess its warning on the SSL certificate. Is there a way, I can ignore the certificate checks?

I build the jar by follow the readme,
and then run it in pycharm https://www.dropbox.com/s/qmrs72l0p8p4bc2/Screen%20Shot%202016-07-06%20at%2011.40.26%20PM.png?dl=0

line 11 is     x = tfs.block(df, "x")

code:
```
import tensorflow as tf
import tensorframes as tfs
from pyspark.shell import sqlContext
from pyspark.sql import Row

data = [Row(x=float(x)) for x in range(10)]
df = sqlContext.createDataFrame(data)

with tf.Graph().as_default() as g:
    # The TensorFlow placeholder that corresponds to column 'x'.
    # The shape of the placeholder is automatically inferred from the DataFrame.
    x = tfs.block(df, "x")
    # The output that adds 3 to x
    z = tf.add(x, 3, name='z')
    # The resulting dataframe
    df2 = tfs.map_blocks(z, df)

# The transform is lazy as for most DataFrame operations. This will trigger it:
df2.collect()

issue_title: Using sas_clients queue when connecting to SAS Grid
issue_body:
Hello saspy team,

**Context:** 
We are using `saspy` and `sas_kernel` to connect to a SAS Grid. I was able to follow the setup instructions - thank you for making it so straight forward! - and now I can connect and submit code to the grid.

When I check the grid jobs, I noticed the LSF job started through saspy is running in the `normal` queue instead of the `sas_clients` queue. I think the wrinkle here is that the SASApp Workspace is setup to use the SASApp Grid Server to launch the LSF jobs. Currently there is NO default queue setup there and the queue the SAS clients get is based on an "option set". The option sets available are:

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/2080150/33023252-8415fc32-cdd5-11e7-9a28-87ab4d2b2741.png)

Since none of those are Jupyter, we are getting the default queue for the SASApp server context which is currently the "normal" queue. 

**Question:**
Is there any way to specify the `Grid Option Set` in the `sascfg.py` configuration to pass through to the IOM server and on down to LSF. If not it would seem that the only way to force that to happen would be to create an app server context for Jupyter and force it to have a default queue of sas_clients. I'm assuming that would work, but we would have to test and ideally would want to avoid that configuration overhead if possible.

Thanks!

issue_title: Reading the logs from the running container in the parent application
issue_body:
Hello,

I try to use Docker as a base layer for my packaging system and Docker Python API as a management utility. 

Consider the situation when we have a container with a long-running and verbose build process (load dependencies -> compile sources -> build packages) that writes a lot of things into stdout or possibly stderr. This container is launched inside the Python process and I want to translate the output of build container to the parent's stdout in a non-blocking regime: 

```python
In [11]: g = client.attach('30501a3afa15a7d3ed6dbe4add5cfb5abac2ebef98a21a791635c9d041ce269a', stream=True, stdout=True, stderr=True, logs=True)

# This hangs and print nothing...
In [12]: for line in g: print line
...
KeyboardInterrupt

# However, the logs are not empty:
In [13]: client.logs('30501a3afa15a7d3ed6dbe4add5cfb5abac2ebef98a21a791635c9d041ce269a', stdout=True, stderr=True)
# Prints a lots of logs...
```
How can I access the logs 'online'?.. Any help will be appreciated.

Follow-up to #390.

In short, trying to do something like this with `docker-py`:
```
$docker run <...>
Name: <user input here>
```

I'm not able to get user input with `docker-py`, is this possible? Adding `stdin_open = True, tty = True` does not print the input prompt (like `Name:` above) - merely drops me into a shell where commands do nothing:

```
$<run my docker-py stuff>

why
no
prompt?
```

So, how can I prompt for and pass along user input?

issue_title: Split EmbeddingComposite into FindEmbeddingComposite and ApplyEmbeddingComposite
issue_body:
A user may want to only use the part of the `EmbeddingComposite` layer that handles seamlessly embedding and unembedding, but not calculate a new embedding each time. They may already have an embedding or they may want to use another tool (other than `minorminer`) to calculate their embedding.

As well, this logic is duplicated in `VirtualGraph`. Having the embed --> sample --> unembed workflow in one place would make it easier to update in the future.
should all be `pmodel` or `p_model` or similar. Not `widget`.

issue_title: simple_tagger tutorial error: use all_predictions.data.cpu().numpy() instead of all_predictions.cpu().numpy()?
issue_body:
Running through the Training and Evaluating Models tutorial using the default pre-built docker image results in an ```AttributeError: 'Variable' object has no attribute 'numpy'``` for:
```
/stage/allennlp/models/simple_tagger.py in decode(self, output_dict)
    124         all_predictions = output_dict['class_probabilities']
    125         if not isinstance(all_predictions, numpy.ndarray):
--> 126             all_predictions = all_predictions.cpu().numpy()
    127         if all_predictions.ndim == 3:
    128             predictions_list = [all_predictions[i] for i in range(all_predictions.shape[0])]

Does the tensor need to be pulled out of the variable first with a ```.data``` method call before it can be converted to a numpy array? issue_title: PEI client does not conform to POSIX-style timezone names
issue_body:
This POSIX-style timezone name declared in `pei.py` is wrong:

```
    TZ_NAME = 'Etc/GMT-4'  # Times are always given in Atlantic Standard Time
```

They are inverse from my initial ISO-8601 expectations. See https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tz_database#Area

Fix the timezone, but also investigate how I could have missed this in the unit tests, which _are_ checking timestamps.

issue_title: Deis limits isn't as permissive as it could be
issue_body:
[nathan@omega hedgehog]$ deis limits:set web=1024MB worker=512MB
Applying limits... 400 BAD REQUEST
{u'memory': [u'Limit format: <number><unit>, where unit = B, K, M or G']}

The error format isn't also very nice...

Currently deis is using:
```
DEIS_DATETIME_FORMAT = '%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S%Z'

const DEIS_DATETIME_FORMAT string = "2006-01-02T15:04:05MST"
```

ISO8601:
```
DEIS_DATETIME_FORMAT = '%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S%z'

const DEIS_DATETIME_FORMAT string = "2006-01-02T15:04:05Z0700"
```

ISO8601 expects UTC be represented by 'Z', Go supports this, however Pythons strftime does not which makes the example above not 100% correct. I think instead we'd need to call .isoformat() - which kinda breaks the pattern of defining time format in one spot.

This would make it a little simpler for log ingestion etc.

Would this adversely affect existing clusters?

issue_title: Speed up tests suite on Travis
issue_body:
Currently the Travis build takes way to long.

Also, our tests 

- are not parallelized (we had memeory issues that forced us to limit number of CPUs to 1)
- are not sorted into unit tests, integrations tests and notebook tests.

Doing both should improve the runtime of a travis run and allow more agile development.

see also

https://docs.travis-ci.com/user/speeding-up-the-build/

issue_title: issue with cyrillic field names and pandas
issue_body:
Hi there,

I have a piece of code that is used to load sql query results into pandas dataframe:

    import turbodbc
    import pandas as pd
    
    connect = turbodbc.connect(dsn='mydsn')
    df = pd.read_sql('select * from table1',connect)

The problem is that if in the table (or view like in my case) there are cyrillic symbols **in the field names**, when I try to load results into the df I get error 

> 'utf-8' codec can't decode byte 0xce in position 0: invalid
> continuation byte.

This error appears both with prefer_unicode=True and without.

Recent version of pyodbc (4.0.16) works alright, but pyodbc 3.0.7 (the one that is included into anacoda by default) has the same issue.

Is it possible to fix it without changing the field names?

issue_title: Use X-API-Sign header to set sign and POST commands in request body
issue_body: This is supported by Centrifugo for a long time and results in more performant API request handling (about 30% faster when no need to parse Form). Also I think it's better to utilize requests lib instead of urllib as requests already standard de facto for Python.

Isomorphism still relies on unnormalized predicate strings. Compare the following where `s1` and `s2` only differ by the presence of `_rel` at the end of the predicates:

```python
>>> from delphin.mrs import simplemrs, compare
>>> s1
'[ LTOP: h0 INDEX: e2 [ e SF: prop TENSE: past MOOD: indicative PROG: - PERF: - ] RELS: < [ proper_q<0:6> LBL: h4 ARG0: x3 [ x PERS: 3 NUM: sg IND: + ] RSTR: h5 BODY: h6 ]  [ named<0:6> LBL: h7 CARG: "Abrams" ARG0: x3 ]  [ _sleep_v_1<7:13> LBL: h1 ARG0: e2 ARG1: x3 ] > HCONS: < h0 qeq h1 h5 qeq h7 > ]'
>>> s2
'[ LTOP: h0 INDEX: e2 [ e SF: prop TENSE: past MOOD: indicative PROG: - PERF: - ] RELS: < [ proper_q_rel<0:6> LBL: h4 ARG0: x3 [ x PERS: 3 NUM: sg IND: + ] RSTR: h5 BODY: h6 ]  [ named_rel<0:6> LBL: h7 CARG: "Abrams" ARG0: x3 ]  [ _sleep_v_1_rel<7:13> LBL: h1 ARG0: e2 ARG1: x3 ] > HCONS: < h0 qeq h1 h5 qeq h7 > ]\n\n'
>>> x1 = simplemrs.loads_one(s1)
>>> x2 = simplemrs.loads_one(s2)
>>> x1 == x2  # generally a stricter test than isomorphism
True
>>> compare.isomorphic(x1,x2)
False
>>> compare.isomorphic(x1,x1)
True
>>> compare.isomorphic(x2,x2)
True
```

`compare.isomorphic()` should use `pred.short_form()` instead of `pred.string`

The `--tsdb-stdout` option for ACE gives a lot more info than the current stdout protocol, so it can be used instead. This is a new feature, so pyDelphin should test for errors and back off to the default protocol in case of failures.

As a bonus, using this feature would allow pyDelphin to create [incr tsdb()] profiles (see #58).

issue_title: DMRX creates <realpred> nodes without required pos field
issue_body:
Jacy has a predicate like `_te_adjunct_rel`, which PyDelphin converts to the following realpred:

```xml
<realpred lemma="te" sense="adjunct" />
```

Then dmrx._decode_pred returns `None` for `elem.get('pos')`, and Pred.realpred trips on the non-string value of the pos field.

See: http://lists.delph-in.net/archives/developers/2017/002631.html

issue_title: Quantifier checks not always looking for RSTR role.
issue_body:
Pred, ElementaryPredication, and Node components all have an `is_quantifier()` method, but only in ElementaryPredication does it look for the RSTR role on its arguments (because only EPs have arguments as part of the data structure).

Where the information is available (e.g. in converting from MRS to DMRS), the RSTR role should only be used in determining if something is a quantifier.

We should possibly also deprecate `is_quantifier()` on Node and Pred, and instead use something like `xmrs.nodeids(quantifier=True)` in order to get this information. If someone wants to use the generally-but-not-necessarily-true assumption that a Node or Pred with `q` as the pos value is a quantifier, they can look directly at `pred.pos`.

issue_title: Confusing error message from ItsdbProfile.join() on disjoint tables
issue_body:
Running `ItsdbProfile.join()` on tables that don't share a key gives a confusing error message:

```python
>>> list(p.join('item', 'result'))
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "/home/goodmami/repos/pydelphin/delphin/itsdb.py", line 634, in join
    key = keys.pop()
KeyError: 'pop from an empty set'
```

If no shared key exists, a suitable ItsdbError should be raised (e.g. "Cannot join tables; no shared key exists.")

issue_title: uninstall should be available as an alias for remove
issue_body:
Maybe it's just me, but because the command to install something with conda is `install`. I always type `conda uninstall` and then get confused why it doesn't work. Could we please have `uninstall` as an alias for `remove`?

To me it would make sense if it were `{'add', 'remove'}` or `{'install', 'uninstall'}`. The mix seems counterintuitive to me.

I've been having an issue with conda authenticating with our companies proxy server.  If I set the HTTP_PROXY and HTTPS_PROXY environment variables with the username and password it works fine, but saving my password in plain sight isn't a viable option.  The conda configuration states:

"Note: If you do not include the username and password, of if authentication fails, conda will prompt for a username and password."

So I tried setting the proxies without the username and password in my .condarc, but get the following problem:

.condarc
```
# Proxy settings: http://[username]:[password]@[server]:[port]
proxy_servers:
    http: http://proxy.company.com:8080
    https: https://proxy.company.com:8080
ssl_verify: true
```
![image](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/9438047/17027116/dd9579d0-4f1f-11e6-85a0-e0a688f71182.png)

First, my password is still in plain sight, and second, there seems to be a bug since it prints `https proxy username:` again after I've typed the username but before the `Password:` prompt. Once I hit enter after typing my password, it prompts `https proxy username:` again and as soon as I touch any key it prints the `Password:` prompt.

A fix or any help would be much appreciated.

Thanks.

issue_title: conda tries to downgrade even though it seems to not be necessary
issue_body:

issue_title: new type of package dependence: dependence on executables only
issue_body:
When package  A depends on package B, but only needs from package B some executable files/scripts (and not libraries or include files), then package B can be installed in a separate conda environment.   Then any conflicts between B and its dependencies, and any other packages needed by A, can be completely avoided.  The separate conda environment would be put under the main conda environment (say under etc/env_B ).   For each executable in package B, say etc/env_B/bin/myprog , we create an executable script in the main conda environment (  bin/myprog ) which temporarily activates B's conda environment etc/env_B , then runs the original executable in etc/env_B/bin/myprog, then deactivates B's conda environment.

Thus, for example, if the package A needs python 3, it can still depend on a package that needs python 2, as long as it only needs executables from that package and not libraries.

To implement this, a new type of 'requirements' would be added to meta.xml; say, 'requirements-executables' .

issue_title: Misaligned package sizes in download output
issue_body:
**I'm submitting a...** - [x] bug report - [ ] feature request ## Current Behavior During the "Downloading and Extracting Packages" phase of `conda update `, `conda` displays each package, its download size, and its progress bar. When printing the download sizes, It seems to only provide enough space for a four-character long file size, excluding the byte unit (e.g. `12.4 MB`). A five character file size, such as `149.6 MB`, causes the pipe separating the size from the progress bar to misaligned with the preceding and following rows. issue_title: ENH: add --force option to conda env create
issue_body:
Both conda env create and conda create need a --force option that will overwrite the environment if it already exists.

conda could warn the user and prompt for confirmation but must also allow for --yes option to not prompt so conda can be used effectively in automation scripts that won't have human interaction.

issue_title: --update-dependencies doesn't seem to have any effect
issue_body:
Here is a case where ``--update-depdencies`` doesn't seem to do anything:

```
$ conda create -n debug python=2.7
Fetching package metadata ...............
Solving package specifications: .

[snip]

$ source activate debug
$ conda install -c glueviz glue-core
Fetching package metadata ...
............
Solving package specifications: .

[snip]
```

I now force install an older version of PyQt5:

```
$ conda install pyqt=5.6.0=py27_1
Fetching package metadata ...............
Solving package specifications: .

Package plan for installation in environment /Users/tom/miniconda3/envs/debug:

The following packages will be DOWNGRADED:

    pyqt: 5.6.0-py27hf21fe59_6 --> 5.6.0-py27_1

Proceed ([y]/n)? y
```

and now install ``glue-core`` again, expecting this would update any dependencies.

```
$ conda install -c glueviz glue-core --update-dependencies
Fetching package metadata ...............
Solving package specifications: .

# All requested packages already installed.
# packages in environment at /Users/tom/miniconda3/envs/debug:
#
glue-core                 0.12.2                   py27_0    glueviz
```

But nothing gets updated. However, ``update --all`` works as expected:

```
$ conda update --all
Fetching package metadata ...............
Solving package specifications: .

Package plan for installation in environment /Users/tom/miniconda3/envs/debug:

The following packages will be UPDATED:

    pyqt: 5.6.0-py27_1 --> 5.6.0-py27hf21fe59_6

Proceed ([y]/n)? 
```

Note that:

```
$ conda update -c glueviz glue-core --update-dependencies
```

doesn't work either. I would expect that dependencies would get updated in either case.

issue_body:
Part of my conda environment setup uses this line:

    conda install -y libiconv=1.14=0 libxml2=2.9.4=0 libxslt=1.1.28=3 lxml=3.4.0=py27_0 numpy=1.8.2 openssl=1.0.2j=0 py=1.4.31=py27_0 pytest=2.9.2=py27_0 pytz=2016.10=py27_0 readline=6.2=2 scikit-learn=0.14.1=np18py27_1 setuptools=27.2.0=py27_0 sqlite=3.13.0=0 tk=8.5.18=0 wheel=0.29.0=py27_0 zlib=1.2.8=3  python-snappy=0.5.1

Has been in place for over a month.  Chef runs 2-3 times  a day and this line is run each time. In experimenting, I moved `python-snappy=0.5.1` to it's own conda install line. It added dependencies.  here is the history from conda:

You can see that there is a change dependencies.  You can see on Jan 26, it was installed.  Why when I moved the package to a seperate line did it change the installed packages.

This can from an investigation around this general issue.  Took my environment script and as you can see I nail all the versions of the packages.  One example I was chasing down. Where as older systems had libgfortran 0.1, my testing this week, scripy requires libgfortran 0.3 and fails because it cannot find libgfortran 0.1.

issue_title: [Bug] File system view is reloaded even when switching between files with same file path
issue_body:
I've opened two files from the same directory. When switching between them the "file system view" is refreshed. As the files are on a network share with lots of files in the directory this needs some time.

The even better implementation would be to first get the "new" file path and checking if it differs from the file path of the current file system view - if not, there's no need to reload it at all.

issue_title: Vunit suppresses modelsim fatal error output
issue_body:
If modelsim exits with some fatal error, VUnit does not print the error output from modelsim. It appears to the user as if the tests are failing, when in fact modelsim fails due to a missing LM_LICENSE_FILE.

If the simulator process returns some unknown error code, please also print the output and, if it can be distinguished from a test failure, exit with a fatal error.

    $ python run.py --log-level debug
    Starting lib.tb_file_reader.test_input_file
      DEBUG - Started process with pid=63631: '/.../modeltech/linux/vsim -c -l /home/drizzd/.../vunit_out/modelsim/transcript0'
      DEBUG - Process with pid=63631 terminated with code=4
    fail (P=0 S=0 F=1 T=1) lib.tb_file_reader.test_input_file (0.1 seconds)
    
      DEBUG - TestRunner: Leaving
      DEBUG - Process with pid=63631 terminated with code=4
    ==== Summary ==============================================
    fail lib.tb_file_reader.test_input_file (0.1 seconds)
    ===========================================================
    pass 0 of 1
    fail 1 of 1
    ===========================================================
    Total time was 0.1 seconds
    Elapsed time was 0.1 seconds
    ===========================================================
    Some failed!

The `verilog_parser` argument in the constructor of class `Project` is initialized with an instance of class `VerilogParser`. Already the import of this class causes that the `VerilogParser`/`Tokenizer` constructor is called. However, if an instance of the `VUnit` class is created by the user, the constructor of the `Project` class initializes the `VerilogParser` and hence the `Tokenizer` class again. This causes that all tokens exist twice inside the compiled regular expression object.

issue_title: delete method of Elasticsearch class can delete mapping or index
issue_body:
if id argument of delete method is either of None , '' , () or [] , then mapping will be deleted.

delete method invokes _make_path function which creates a URL string from parts of path provided.
It skips a part if it is in (None , '' , () or [] ) and returns the URL string formed. 
delete method then invokes perform_request method with "DELETE" method . 

The document says "Delete a typed JSON document from a specific index based on its id." . But, unknowingly user can delete the mapping in the elasticsearch index.

Body decoding in https://github.com/elastic/elasticsearch-py/blob/master/elasticsearch/connection/base.py#L78-L79 should only be done if we are actually going to use it in the logs by either `log_request_success` or `_log_trace`

**Problem:**
Operations involving type *login_server* fail due to `AttributeError: 'bool' object has no attribute 'rstrip'`

Not limited to `az acr login` -- see example below for `az acr repository list`.

Confirmed on Windows, Linux, and in the Docker container.

**Examples (Windows):**

```
C:\Users\marsma>az acr login --name acr604
'bool' object has no attribute 'rstrip'
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "C:\Program Files (x86)\Microsoft SDKs\Azure\CLI2\lib\site-packages\knack\cli.py", line 193, in invoke
    cmd_result = self.invocation.execute(args)
  File "C:\Program Files (x86)\Microsoft SDKs\Azure\CLI2\lib\site-packages\azure\cli\core\commands\__init__.py", line 319, in execute
    six.reraise(*sys.exc_info())

issue_title: [Storage] Resource group should be optional or omitted for most storage management commands
issue_body: Since storage accounts have globally unique names, there is no reason you should have to specify a resource group name when performing storage account management operations (except create). In KeyVault, the vault is found using only the name provided it is contained within the subscription. If resource group IS specified, it is treated as a filter (i.e. perform this operation only if the vault with X name is in Y resource group).
e.g. `vm/vmss --ip-forwarding (true|false)`

Hi,

I was wondering when will be available the support of File Share service in Storage accounts via the Azure Python SDK

The File Share service is available in the .NET SDK:
http://azure.microsoft.com/en-in/documentation/articles/storage-dotnet-how-to-use-files/

In the meantime, I created a fork of the Azure SDK for Python that support the creation and deletion of file shares in Storage accounts. You can find the fork at:
https://github.com/dominoFire/azure-sdk-for-python

An example for creating a File Share is showed below:

```python
from azure.servicemanagement import *
from azure.storage.fileshareservice import FileShareService

# simple predicates over request status 
# You can find the complete test at
# https://bitbucket.org/dominofire/sweeper
from sweeper.cloud.azure.manager import wait_for_storage_account, wait_for_request_succeeded

#Azure subscription ID
subscription_id = '33232-...'
# path to .pem file that includes the certificate to the Azure Account
pem_file = 'cert.pem'
 
sms = ServiceManagementService(subscription_id, pem_file)

# For viewing the HTTP requests
# azure.http.httpclient.DEBUG_REQUESTS = True
# azure.http.httpclient.DEBUG_RESPONSES = True

res_name = 'creditgeometry'
file_share_name = 'myshare'
storage_account_result = sms.create_storage_account(service_name=res_name,
                                                    description='Storage account for Test {}'.format(res_name),
                                                    label=res_name,
                                                    geo_replication_enabled=None,
                                                    account_type='Standard_LRS',
                                                    location='West US')
wait_for_request_succeeded(storage_account_result.request_id)
wait_for_storage_account(res_name)
storage_keys = sms.get_storage_account_keys(service_name=res_name)
file_svc = FileShareService(account_name=res_name, account_key=storage_keys.storage_service_keys.primary)
file_svc.create_file_share(file_share_name, fail_on_exist=True)
file_svc.delete_file_share(file_share_name, fail_not_exist=True)
sms.delete_storage_account(res_name)
```

issue_title: Update documentation to properly demonstrate how to pass args to a hook
issue_body:
The site at `pre-commit.com` says:

```
If you are writing your own custom hook as a script-type or even a system hook, your hook should expect to receive the args value and then a list of staged files.

For example, assuming your .pre-commit-config.yaml was like below

-   repo: git://github.com/path/to/your/hook/repo
    sha: 5541a6a046b7a0feab73a21612ab5d94a6d3f6f0
    hooks:
    -   id: my-hook-script-id
        args: [--myarg1=1 --myarg1=2]
When you next run pre-commit, it will pass on those args as follows:

path/to/script-or-system-call --myarg1=1 --myarg1=2 dir/file1 dir/file2 file3
If the args property is empty or not defined, you should expect your script to be called:

 path/to/script-or-system-call dir/file1 dir/file2 file3 
```

That's incorrect. The `.pre-commit-config.yaml` file should say

```
...
-   id: my-hook-script-id
    args: [--myarg1=1, --myarg1=2]
```

Note the missing comma. Without the comma the two args will be quoted and passed in as a single arg with a space in the middle. Many programs will fail to parse this.

[SwiftLint](/Realm/SwiftLint) is a great example of a Swift-based linter that has support for [SwiftPM](/apple/swift-package-manager). It'd be great if pre-commit had support to pull down a repo that has a Package.swift, run `swift build`, and install the specified targets to be used as hooks.

issue_title: Add property with that indicates if queue is closed
issue_body: In some cases it is useful to know if queue is closed, I think we need to add something like `queue.closed` property.

I run this
```
#!/usr/bin/env python3
import time
from threading import Thread
from itertools import count
import asyncio
import janus


class Timer():
    def __init__(self):
        self._start = None

    def start(self):
        self._start = time.time()

    def stop(self):
        return time.time() - self._start


def consumer_thread(taskq, result):
    while True:
        task = taskq.get()
        if task is None:
            return
        result[0] = task


async def producer_thread(taskq):
    for x in range(10000 + 1):
        await taskq.put(x)
    await taskq.put(None)


def bench_sync_async():
    taskq = janus.Queue(maxsize=100)

    loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()

    result = [None]
    consumer = Thread(target=consumer_thread, args=[taskq.sync_q, result])
    consumer.start()

    loop.run_until_complete(producer_thread(taskq.async_q))
    consumer.join()

    assert result[0] == 10000


def main(**kwargs):
    timer = Timer()

Closing request/session is too error-prone in the case, especially if an exception was generated.

The only robust solution is prohibiting `await request()` syntax but supporting `async with request() as resp:` only.

After implementing proposed restriction we'll get very clean context and behavior.

## Long story short

It seems that aiohttp rounds up epoch times and timeouts using `math.ceil`, effecitvely preventing timeouts of less than 1 second.

## Expected behaviour

Subsecond timeouts should be supported, at least at the millisecond level.

## Actual behaviour

Subsecond timeouts are not respected.

## Steps to reproduce

I stumbled on this issue while trying unsuccessfully to write a unit test that tests timeouts, until I came across some related tests in aiohttp itself that [patch `aiohttp.helpers.ceil` to be a no-op](https://github.com/aio-libs/aiohttp/blob/master/tests/test_client_functional.py#L580). Without the patch these tests (usually) fail.

## Your environment

`aiohttp==2.0.7`

Add feature for generating urls to static files with version hash to have more control over caching in browser.

## Expected behaviour
When you resolve url from `StaticResource` you can use `append_version` flag to add a hash of file (version) into url, which will change if file content changed and than for browser it's will be new url (which not exists in cache and will be reloaded)

## Example
```html
<script src="{{ url('static', filename='build/js/bundle.js', append_version=True) }}"></script>
```
with `append_version` flag `StaticResource` will append `v` param into url query string  with hash of the file
```
/static/build/js/bundle.js?v=aDc7UcFqFqXFUOYI8crlpxDAW-i41AGSNdDP8eQebSM%3D
```

issue_title: aiomysql.sa example fails with "TypeError: 'ResultProxy' object is not iterable" since 0.0.15
issue_body:
Hi! 
I was trying to run the example from http://aiomysql.readthedocs.io/en/latest/sa.html :

```python
import asyncio
import sqlalchemy as sa

from aiomysql.sa import create_engine


metadata = sa.MetaData()

tbl = sa.Table('tbl', metadata,
               sa.Column('id', sa.Integer, primary_key=True),
               sa.Column('val', sa.String(255)))


@asyncio.coroutine
def go():
    engine = yield from create_engine(user='root', 
                                      db='test_pymysql',
                                      host='127.0.0.1',
                                      password='')

    with (yield from engine) as conn:
        yield from conn.execute(tbl.insert().values(val='abc'))

        res = yield from conn.execute(tbl.select())
        for row in res:
            print(row.id, row.val)

asyncio.get_event_loop().run_until_complete(go())
```
and got the following:
```
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
TypeError                                 Traceback (most recent call last)
<ipython-input-2-4a46c1f5df04> in <module>()
     26             print(row.id, row.val)
     27 
---> 28 asyncio.get_event_loop().run_until_complete(go())

~/miniconda3/lib/python3.6/asyncio/base_events.py in run_until_complete(self, future)
    466             raise RuntimeError('Event loop stopped before Future completed.')
    467 
--> 468         return future.result()
    469 
    470     def stop(self):

<ipython-input-2-4a46c1f5df04> in go()
     23 
     24         res = yield from conn.execute(tbl.select())
---> 25         for row in res:
     26             print(row.id, row.val)
     27 

TypeError: 'ResultProxy' object is not iterable
```

replacing  ```for row in res:``` with something like
```for row in (yield from res.fetchall()):```
fixes the issue. A little exploration revealed that this error comes since 0.0.15 release and probably since this commit: https://github.com/aio-libs/aiomysql/commit/83252b36e3476779d98059119dec3c4140a7befe so I believe that the example should be brought in line with current code.
Since project under active development lets agree on code style and check it on each PR. Should be relatively easy to do.
Need pass event loop to docker to use one main event loop for the daemon.

We just ran into this due to: https://github.com/aio-libs/aiohttp/issues/1954.  Here until we have a resolution to that issue.  To work around this we'd have to disable read_timeout, and re-impl read() to have a timeout per chunk.

issue_title: Clear information from database when running nbgrader assign
issue_body:
If a notebook name is changed, for example, then the database will become inconsistent if `nbgrader assign` is run before and after. We should clear information from the database for an assignment before running `nbgrader assign`.

The one catch is that if there are submissions already recorded in the database, we don't want to clear information about an assignment. In this case, we should perhaps check whether running `nbgrader assign` would add or remove anything to the database (e.g. beyond just changing cell contents), and throw an error.

issue_title: Output area height is rounded when fixing height on clear
issue_body: In https://github.com/jupyter/notebook/blob/5192d72c63369d7167c5472d06f87072d68ecc53/notebook/static/notebook/js/outputarea.js#L969, we use jquery to find and fix the height of an output area in order to prevent jumpiness when clearing an output area. However, the jquery `.height()` function rounds the height to the nearest integer, which causes some jitter. It would be better to use `.getBoundingClientRect().height`, which doesn't round the height.

issue_body:
As in the subject: allow something like:

    class Song(Model):
        ...
        class Options:
            serialize_only_dirty=True

The problem is, my API allows clearing fields by setting them to `null`. So, by default all the (even non-required) fields are converted and thus cleared and I cannot use `serialize_when_none`, because then nulls wouldn't get through at all.

   ...: 
   ...: 

In [3]: x = np.array([ 0.0667,  0.944 ,  1.42  ,  1.7165,  2.177 ])

In [4]: for end in range(x.size, 0, -1):
   ...:     assert_almost_equal(np.diff(x[:end]), diff(x[:end]))
   ...: 
In [5]: 
```
The definitions of the gradients *should* therefore be the same but differ when `x` is of size 1:
```python
In [8]: g1 = elementwise_grad(np.diff)
   ...: g2 = elementwise_grad(diff)
   ...: for end in range(x.size, 0, -1):
   ...:     assert_almost_equal(g1(x[:end]), g2(x[:end]))
   ...: 
<snip>
AssertionError: 
Arrays are not almost equal to 7 decimals

(shapes (0,), (1,) mismatch)
 x: array([], dtype=float64)
 y: array([ 0.])
```
Looking more closely at the arguments:
```python
In [9]: x = x[:1]
   ...: x
Out[9]: array([ 0.0667])

In [10]: np.diff(x)
Out[10]: array([], dtype=float64)

In [11]: g2(x)
Out[11]: array([ 0.])

In [12]: g1(x)
Out[12]: array([], dtype=float64)
```
So `autograd`'s automatic differentiation of the `diff` function gives `array([ 0.])` for a 1-element input vector whereas the gradient of `np.diff` gives an empty array `array([], dtype=float64)`

issue_body:
To replicate this problem:
```python
In [1]: import autograd.numpy as np

In [2]: from autograd.test_util import check_grads

In [3]: check_grads(np.rollaxis, modes=['rev'])(np.random.randn(1, 2, 3), -1)

~/dev/autograd/autograd/test_util.py in check_vjp(f, x)
     29
     30     vjp_y = x_vs.covector(vjp(y_vs.covector(y_v)))
---> 31     assert vspace(vjp_y) == x_vs
     32     vjv_numeric = x_vs.inner_prod(x_v, vjp_y)
     33     vjv_exact   = y_vs.inner_prod(y_v, jvp(x_v))

AssertionError:

This was silently giving incorrect gradients in my code, a bug which was not easy to track down! This would have been picked up by vspace checking, so maybe we should write a `vspace_checking_grad` operator which enables vspace checks for every primitive.

Although there are some rough edges with installing workflows from the Tool Shed (e.g. http://dev.list.galaxyproject.org/Installing-workflows-from-the-Tool-Shed-is-very-confusing-tc4666888.html ) the ability for the workflow author to include a ``repository_dependencies.xml `` file ought to help with ensuring the workflow dependencies can be automatically installed by a Galaxy Administrator.

In principle, by parsing ``*.ga`` file (or set of files if you wanted to publish several workflows in a single ToolShed repository), the ``repository_dependencies.xml`` file could be auto generated - and this might be a useful feature for planemo.

(Alternatively this kind of logic might be better in the Tool Shed code, or in Galaxy's ``*.ga`` import code?).

For example with https://github.com/peterjc/galaxy_blast/tree/master/workflows/blast_top_hit_species I created the ``repository_dependencies.xml`` by hand, turning these lines in the ``*.ga`` file:

```
            "tool_id": "toolshed.g2.bx.psu.edu/repos/peterjc/sample_seqs/sample_seqs/0.2.1", 
            "tool_id": "toolshed.g2.bx.psu.edu/repos/devteam/ncbi_blast_plus/ncbi_blastx_wrapper/0.1.01", 
            "tool_id": "toolshed.g2.bx.psu.edu/repos/devteam/fasta_to_tabular/fasta2tab/1.1.0", 
            "tool_id": "toolshed.g2.bx.psu.edu/repos/bgruening/unique/bg_uniq/0.3", 
```

into:

```
    <repository owner="devteam" name="ncbi_blast_plus" />
    <repository owner="devteam" name="fasta_to_tabular" />
    <repository owner="peterjc" name="sample_seqs" />
    <repository owner="bgruening" name="unique" />
```

Upon upload to the Tool Shed as https://toolshed.g2.bx.psu.edu/view/peterjc/blast_top_hit_species these were filled in to point at the latest tool revisions (which at the time would have been the right version numbers):

```
    <repository changeset_revision="2fe07f50a41e" name="ncbi_blast_plus" owner="devteam" toolshed="https://toolshed.g2.bx.psu.edu" />
    <repository changeset_revision="9d189d08f2ad" name="fasta_to_tabular" owner="devteam" toolshed="https://toolshed.g2.bx.psu.edu" />
    <repository changeset_revision="02c13ef1a669" name="sample_seqs" owner="peterjc" toolshed="https://toolshed.g2.bx.psu.edu" />
    <repository changeset_revision="7ce75adb93be" name="unique" owner="bgruening" toolshed="https://toolshed.g2.bx.psu.edu" />
```

Note that a single workflow may contain tools installed from multiple Tool Shed repositories, but at the time of writing the ``repository_dependencies.xml`` file can only be used for dependencies within the same Tool Shed. Assuming the dependencies were from the same Tool Shed, then the revisions could be examined for suitable ``changeset_revision`` entries providing the desired version string of the tool.

issue_title: Drop the inner Meta classes on entities
issue_body:
The inner Meta classes are arguably a bad idea. A class should contain a bundle of data and logic that operates on that data. However, the Entity classes contain only data. By this reasoning, it is better to drop the Meta classes and add a regular class attribute to hold the same data.

I suggest creating a class attribute named `_meta`. I suggest that this class attribute be private for the following reasons:

* If `Meta`/`_meta` is changed, then *all* entity classes are affected. This is only something that should be done by someone who is an expert with NailGun and who really knows what they're doing.
* Experience with Robottelo shows that access to meta is unnecessary. The only modules that touch the current Meta class are `robottelo.entities` and `tests.foreman.api.test_multiple_path`. The former will soon be ported to NailGun, so those cases of access can be controlled. It is questionable whether the accesses to Meta in the latter case are really necessary.

This is a breaking change because the inner `Meta` class is a public attribute.

Documentation is not being generated on Read The Docs. The relevant error looks like this:

      File "conf.py", line 10, in <module>
        from pkg_resources import SetuptoolsVersion
    ImportError: cannot import name SetuptoolsVersion

`pkg_resources.SetuptoolsVersion` is provided by the `setuptools` package, which NailGun lists as a dependency. The problem is that RTD downgrades the installed setuptools version from 0.16.0 to 1.1.

    Downloading/unpacking setuptools==1.1

Newer versions of setuptools, such as 0.16 (installed by default before the downgrade) and 0.18 (the latest) do provide the `SetuptoolsVersion` class.

There are two possible solutions I see:

* Specify a minimum required `setuptools` version. This may or may not fix the issue, depending upon whether the downgrade happens before or after NailGun's dependencies are installed.
* Drop `setuptools` as a dependency and use `packaging` instead. The `packaging` package is provided by the Python Packaging Authority, and it's used under the hood by setuptools. Its `packaging.version.Version` class and `packaging.version.parse` function would serve the same role as the `SetuptoolsVersion` class and `parse_version` functions, respectively. This option is mainly attractive because it's more targeted than setuptools, which provdes boatloads of functionality we don't need.

There are several places in the code, where the values are not checked if they are resources. This means that the code gives wrong results.

Also related to #266

issue_title: Awit use delay Windows server 2012 R2
Request.Path is empty while Request.PathBase is not
issue_body:
# Request.Path is empty while Request.PathBase is not

# General

Using .NET Core 2.0, Windows 10, IIS Express.

While debugging my .NET Core web application, IIS Express launches a new browser window with the address `localhost:7777/dashboard`. Usually, I am able to view my web site at this point, but upon transferring my project over to a new machine with a fresh copy of Visual Studio Community 2017, the project returns a very strange error. The environment is identical to the environment on my last machine, so I see no reason why this error should occur. The error occurs because `HttpContext.Request.Path` is empty, even though I am accessing the path `/dashboard` in the URL. I noticed something very strange while looking into the error. `HttpContext.Request.PathBase` returns `/dashboard` even though `HttpContext.Request.Path` is returning an empty string.

The `HttpContext` object is accessed from the `context` argument passed within the line of code, `app.Run(async (context) =>{});` located in `Startup.cs`.
